title: Berry Stain on Cotton
stainChemistry: Berry stains (blueberry, blackberry, raspberry, strawberry, mulberry) are tannin stains dominated by anthocyanin pigments — the same family that colors red wine. Anthocyanins bond to cotton cellulose tightly. Blueberry and blackberry have the deepest, bluest anthocyanin load; strawberry has a weaker pigment but additional pectin that can make the stain sticky. Cotton is pure cellulose: chemically tolerant of enzymes, acid-side tannin spotters, 3% H₂O₂, and hot post-chemistry wash. Fresh berry stains move easily; aged berry dries into a dark tannin-anthocyanin matrix that needs oxidation to lift.
whyThisWorks: Fresh: cold flush + hot-water pour-through knocks out most of the stain before chemistry. Aged: acid-side tannin spotter disrupts the anthocyanin grip, then 3% H₂O₂ oxidizes the residual color. Cotton's durability lets the full sequence run at pace. The one hard constraint: heat sets aged tannin permanently, so the hot-water pour-through is fresh-only, and the hot final wash only runs after all chemistry is flushed.
defaultAssumption: Most berry stains are caught fresh (within an hour) and are high-confidence DIY on cotton. Aged + dark cotton narrows the oxidizer lane; set expectations accordingly.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Cold running water, white cotton terry towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cold flush from the BACK of the fabric. Blot hard from underneath. The anthocyanin pigment is water-mobile when fresh; hydraulic pressure pushes it out the way it came in. Cold only — hot water on aged stains sets the tannin permanently.

Apply NSD to the stain, sponge gently. NSD suspends pigment so the next steps act on what's bound to the fiber, not what's loose. Blot with a clean white towel. Noticeable lightening should occur before moving on.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Kettle of hot — not boiling — water, catch basin
spottingProtocol.instruction: Only for stains under 30 minutes old. Stretch the fabric taut over a bowl, pour hot water through from the back. Heat breaks grip on fresh tannin. Skip on aged stains: heat sets older tannin. Express lane for fresh, off-limits for aged.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Professional tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter or PRENETT-A) — acid-side tannin treatment
spottingProtocol.instruction: For any residual purple-blue or red shadow, apply an acid-side tannin spotter by dropper. The acid disrupts the anthocyanin-cellulose grip. Dwell 2–5 min, blot. Flip cloth frequently. Repeat once if shadow persists.

Apply 3% H₂O₂ drop-by-drop on residual color. Peroxide oxidizes the anthocyanin conjugated system to colorless. Dwell 3–5 min, blot. Repeat once if needed. Never layer oxidizers — one at a time, NSD-flushed between.

spottingProtocol.dwellTime: Until rinse clear; full wash cycle
spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush with NSD until the rinse runs clear. Launder hot per care label. Cotton is heat-tolerant once the chemistry is flushed.

steps: 1. Cold flush from the back — fresh berry pigment is water-mobile.
2. NSD in cold water. Sponge, dwell 2–3 min, blot.
3. FRESH ONLY (<30 min): hot pour-through. Skip on aged.
4. Professional tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter / PRENETT-A) — dropper, 2–5 min, blot. Repeat once.
5. 3% H₂O₂ drop-by-drop on residual. 3–5 min. One pass. NSD-flush between repeats.
6. NSD flush, hot wash per care label.
products: Professional tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter or PRENETT-A)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold through spotting; hot wash post-chemistry
warnings: Never use hot water on aged berry stains — sets tannin permanently
One oxidizer at a time, NSD-flushed between
Flush all chemistry before heat — residual oxidizer yellows cotton

homeSolutions.instruction: Fresh berry stains come out easy on cotton if you move quickly. Cold water only — hot water on aged or dried berry juice sets the tannin permanently into the fabric. Get to the stain within the first hour if you can.

homeSolutions.agent: Flush From the Back of the Fabric
homeSolutions.instruction: Hold the BACK of the stained area under cold running water for 30–60 seconds. The berry juice pushes out the way it came in. Then blot with a clean white towel from underneath — don't rub.

If the stain is under 30 minutes old: stretch the fabric taut over a bowl, place a clean towel underneath, and pour hot (not boiling) water through from the back. You'll see the stain fade dramatically. Skip this step if the berry's been sitting longer than 30 minutes — heat sets older tannin.

Mix 1 teaspoon Dawn dish soap, 2 tablespoons white vinegar, and 1 cup cold water. Dab onto the stain, let sit 3 minutes, blot from center outward. The soap lifts remaining pigment; the mild acidity of vinegar helps break the berry's anthocyanin bond. Repeat once if color is still visible.
Flush cool water from the reverse side until your blotting towel runs clean. Rinse before the next step — layering oxidizers on top of residue creates a mess.

homeSolutions.agent: OxiClean or 3% Hydrogen Peroxide for Shadows
homeSolutions.instruction: For any lingering purple or pink shadow: dissolve one scoop of OxiClean in 1 cup cool water and soak the stained area 15–30 minutes. Or for a faster spot pass, use 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide on the shadow for 3–5 minutes, then blot. Skip on dark or dyed cotton.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix OxiClean with chlorine bleach, ammonia, or hydrogen peroxide at the same time — one oxidizer at a time.

homeSolutions.agent: Hot Wash, Inspect Before the Dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows. Inspect the stain BEFORE the dryer — any residual tannin sets permanently under dryer heat. If you still see color, repeat the soap/OxiClean round first.

If the berry stain has been through a hot wash + dryer, or two full home rounds don't clear it, take it to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Berry stain on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], treated at home with [dish soap + vinegar / OxiClean / 3% peroxide]."

steps: 1. Move fast — cold water only while the stain is visible.
2. Flush cold from the BACK of the fabric, blot with a clean white towel.
3. Fresh (<30 min) only: hot-water pour-through from the back.
4. Dawn + vinegar + cold water solution: dab, dwell 3 min, blot.
5. Cool rinse from the back until towel runs clean.
6. OxiClean soak OR 3% hydrogen peroxide on residual shadow. Never layer oxidizers.
7. Hot wash per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain has been through a hot wash + dryer, if the cotton is dark dyed and peroxide isn't safe, or if two home rounds haven't cleared it. Take it to a dry cleaner.
warnings: Hot water only for fresh stains (<30 min) — sets older tannin
Never mix OxiClean with chlorine bleach, ammonia, or hydrogen peroxide
Skip peroxide on dark/dyed cotton or inside-seam-test first
Inspect before the dryer — heat sets residual tannin permanently

neverDo: Never layer oxidizers — one at a time, flushed between
Never use hot water on aged berry stains — sets tannin
Never dry with residual chemistry in the fiber — yellows cotton
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates NSD, acid-side tannin spotters, 3% H₂O₂, and hot post-chemistry wash
Dyed cotton: test acid-side spotters and peroxide on inside seam first
Heat-safe fiber once chemistry is cleared

materialWarnings: Hot water only for fresh — sets aged tannin
One oxidizer at a time — flush with NSD between passes
Peroxide on whites; test inside seam on dark cotton

escalation.when: Berry stain heat-set by hot wash or dryer, dark dyed cotton where peroxide isn't safe, or two full home rounds without clearing it.
escalation.whatToTell: "Berry stain on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with [Dawn + vinegar / OxiClean / 3% hydrogen peroxide]. [Washed hot / not yet laundered.]"

contextQuestions: Which berry — blueberry, blackberry, raspberry, strawberry?
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
Has it been through a hot wash or dryer?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Blueberry and blackberry carry the heaviest anthocyanin load — expect an extra peroxide or OxiClean cycle. Strawberry is lighter on pigment but has sticky pectin that needs the soap pass. Heat-set trends toward escalation.
